title: Hofer's Geometry of the Space of Diameters
speaker: Michael Khanevsky

proof_stages.label: Hofer metric and sphere-side motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Hofer length, Hofer norm, and the induced metric on Hamiltonian deformations of a Lagrangian, then frames the open boundedness problem for equators in S^2 via Polterovich's unboundedness result and equivalent half-sphere support questions.

proof_stages.label: Disk diameters and main quantitative theorem
proof_stages.summary: For the unit disk with standard diameter L0, the space of compactly supported Hamiltonian images of L0 is introduced, and the main result is stated: it is Hofer-unbounded and, for nice diameters, the distance to L0 is bounded above linearly in the number of transverse intersections, with sharp linear order.

proof_stages.label: Bigon-elimination upper bound
proof_stages.summary: A constructive algorithm finds a simple bigon between L and L0, pushes it across by a controlled Hamiltonian without creating new intersections, iterates until only a trivial final configuration remains, and thereby gives a linear Hofer-energy upper bound, with a disk refinement improving the constant.

proof_stages.label: Calabi and Calabi-quasimorphism input
proof_stages.summary: The lower-bound machinery is prepared by defining the disk Calabi invariant, homogeneous quasimorphisms, the Entov-Polterovich Calabi quasimorphism on S^2, its pullback to the disk, and the nonzero Hofer-Lipschitz quasimorphism rho obtained as the difference from the ordinary Calabi homomorphism.

proof_stages.label: Coarse descent of rho to diameters
proof_stages.summary: Using the stabilizer of L0, the talk proves that rho vanishes on stabilizer elements after reducing them to commuting half-disk-supported maps, so rho is well-defined on a diameter up to the bounded defect of the quasimorphism.

proof_stages.label: Lower bound, unboundedness, and sharpness
proof_stages.summary: The coarse diameter invariant and Hofer-Lipschitz property give a lower bound for d(phi L0,L0), and a radial rotation family has rho-value and intersection number growing linearly, proving unboundedness and showing the linear upper estimate is sharp in order.

proof_stages.label: Extensions and contrast with the sphere
proof_stages.summary: The same strategy is described for cylinders and curves bounding nondisplaceable disks, including a rotation-number lower bound, while the S^2 case remains mysterious because only one Calabi quasimorphism is known and the speaker can only prove a logarithmic upper bound there.

examples.name: Small-support autonomous bump on the sphere
examples.note: The bump Hamiltonian on S^2 illustrates Polterovich's unbounded one-parameter family in Hofer's metric and motivates the later bounded-neighborhood questions, but the disk proof does not depend on this particular bump.
examples.engagement_reason: The small-support autonomous bump on the sphere is invoked as Polterovich’s known unbounded one-parameter family, with conditions stated but no concrete computation or construction carried out on that bump.

passes.reason: Structurally an ordinary localized autonomous Hamiltonian, but its time maps are used to refute boundedness of Hofer geometry on Ham(S2).
A small-support autonomous bump is an ordinary Hamiltonian, used as a family whose Hofer norm goes to infinity and hence refutes boundedness of Ham(S2).
A small-support autonomous Hamiltonian bump on S2 is an ordinary localized Hamiltonian used to witness failure of Hofer boundedness.
Ordinary localized autonomous Hamiltonian bump whose iterates witness Hofer unboundedness, hence refute boundedness of the Hamiltonian group.
A small-support autonomous bump is an ordinary Hamiltonian used via its time-t maps to witness Hofer unboundedness, hence to refute boundedness of Ham(S2).
an ordinary localized autonomous Hamiltonian bump used to witness failure of boundedness of Hofer geometry on Ham(S2)
The small-support autonomous bump flow is used to witness failure of boundedness of Hofer geometry on Ham(S2), while structurally it is just a localized but otherwise ordinary Hamiltonian.
Used as Polterovich’s witness that Hofer’s metric on Ham(S2) is unbounded, while the localized autonomous bump is not intrinsically degenerate, extremal, exceptional, or maximally symmetric.
Used to exhibit Polterovich’s unbounded Hofer ray, but a small-support autonomous bump is just a localized Hamiltonian, not a sporadic, symmetric, degenerate, or optimal object.
a localized autonomous Hamiltonian bump is used to exhibit Hofer-norm unboundedness of Ham(S²), not as a structurally special object

A small-support autonomous bump Hamiltonian on S2 is used via its time-t maps to witness unbounded Hofer norm, refuting boundedness, but the bump itself is not structurally symmetric, extremal, exceptional, or degenerate.
Used as an unbounded Hofer-norm family witnessing failure of boundedness of Ham(S^2), while a localized autonomous bump is not intrinsically sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric.

examples.name: Typical nice deformed diameter
examples.note: The drawn diameter agreeing with L0 near the boundary and meeting it in finitely many transverse points makes the generic 'nice' condition and the intersection-count estimate concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The deformed nice diameter is drawn and then used in the proof: the speaker finds dgons, pushes them across, tracks disappearing intersections, bounds the energy, and derives the linear estimate.

passes.reason: A generic nice deformed diameter with finite transverse intersections is used as a representative case for the deformation algorithm, not to disprove a claim.
The nice deformed diameter is explicitly a generic transverse representative used to illustrate the estimate and reduction algorithm, not to disprove a claim.
A nice deformed diameter with finitely many transverse intersections is presented as a generic representative for the deformation algorithm, not as a structurally special object or refutation.
A generic nice deformed diameter with finitely many transverse intersections is used as a representative case for the deformation algorithm.
The nice deformed diameter is presented as a generic representative with transverse finite intersections, used for the deformation algorithm rather than to disprove a claim.
a generic nice Hamiltonian-deformed diameter with finite transverse intersections used as a representative case for the deformation algorithm
The nice deformed diameter is presented as a generic illustrative curve with transverse finite intersections, not as an obstruction or structurally special object.

A nice deformed diameter with finitely many transverse intersections is presented as the generic representative case for the estimate/algorithm, not as a failure example or structurally special object.
Introduced as a generic nice Hamiltonian-deformed diameter for the intersection-reduction argument, with no special structural extremality or symmetry.
a nice deformed diameter with finite transverse intersections is presented as a generic representative for the distance estimate

The nice deformed diameter is presented as a generic representative with transverse finite intersections to illustrate the algorithm and estimates, not to disprove a claim or as a structurally special object.
Presented as a generic/nice Hamiltonian-deformed diameter with transverse finite intersections, serving as a representative case rather than an obstruction or structurally special object.
